title: Near-Infrared Observations of BD+44 493 with IGRINS-2
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, accepted for publication on AJ
summary: We present high-resolution (R~45,000), high signal-to-noise (S/N>200) H and K-band spectra of BD+44 493, obtained with the newly commissioned IGRINS-2 instrument on the Gemini North Telescope. BD+44 493 is a well-known carbon-enhanced (CEMP; [C/Fe]=+1.40) ultra metal-poor (UMP; [Fe/H]~-4.0) star that has been extensively studied with high-resolution spectroscopy from the ultraviolet to the infrared. From the IGRINS-2 data, we derive new estimates of the abundances of C, Mg, Si, and Ca by measuring the equivalent widths of several atomic absorption features and comparing the values and line profiles with synthetic models. We measure, for the first time, a weak atomic carbon absorption feature (C I) in a UMP star in the H-band. Our estimated abundance agrees well with other values derived from CH molecules in the optical. The abundance values for the other elements are also in good agreement with previous estimates derived from ultraviolet, optical, and near-infrared observations, confirming the speculations that BD+44 493 is a bona fide second-generation star. With these data, we demonstrate that IGRINS-2 is a powerful new resource for studying and characterizing low-metallicity stars in the Milky Way and beyond.

AI.tldr: This paper presents high-resolution infrared spectra of the ultra metal-poor star BD+44 493 using the new IGRINS-2 instrument on Gemini North. The authors derive abundances of C, Mg, Si, and Ca, including the first detection of a weak atomic carbon line in the H-band for such a star. Results confirm BD+44 493 as a second-generation star and demonstrate IGRINS-2's capability for studying low-metallicity stars.
AI.motivation: To characterize the chemical abundances of the ultra metal-poor star BD+44 493 using the newly commissioned IGRINS-2 instrument and to demonstrate its capabilities for studying low-metallicity stars.
AI.method: High-resolution (R~45,000), high S/N (>200) H and K-band spectra obtained with IGRINS-2 on Gemini North. Abundances of C, Mg, Si, and Ca derived via equivalent width measurements of atomic absorption features and comparison with synthetic models.
AI.result: First detection of a weak atomic carbon (C I) absorption feature in the H-band of an ultra metal-poor star. Derived abundances agree well with previous optical (CH-based) and UV/optical/near-infrared estimates. Confirms BD+44 493 as a bona fide second-generation star.
AI.conclusion: IGRINS-2 is a powerful new resource for studying and characterizing low-metallicity stars in the Milky Way and beyond.

title: Ion-Scale Current Sheets Embedded in Reconnection Jet Shear Layer of the Near-Sun Heliospheric Current Sheet
summary: Context. Magnetic reconnection in the heliospheric current sheet (HCS) plays an important role in restructuring the solar wind magnetic topology and generating plasma jets and magnetic islands. While large-scale signatures of HCS reconnection have been reported in many observational studies, the kinetic-scale structure embedded within reconnection regions remains less well understood. Aims. We investigate the ion-scale currents sheets (CSs) embedded within an HCS reconnection region and their relationship to the flow-shear layer at the edge of a reconnection jet. Methods. We analyzed an HCS crossing observed by the Parker Solar Probe on March 29, 2024, using high-time-resolution magnetic field measurements. We focused on ion-scale magnetic transitions within two brief intervals of flow-shear layer at the edges of the reconnection jet and examined them in a local LMN coordinate system. Results. Twelve representative CSs are identified, whose duration is on average $\sim$0.06 sec, corresponding to spatial scales of only a few ion inertial lengths. They are classified into three types based on the behavior of the out-of-plane magnetic component $B_{M}$: (1) CSs showing clear bipolar $B_{M}$ variations without bifurcation in reconnecting-field ($B_{L}$), (2) CSs with both bipolar $B_{M}$ variations and bifurcated $B_{L}$ profiles characterized by a plateau structure, and (3) CSs where strong fluctuations obscure an otherwise expected bipolar signature. Conclusions. The reconnection jet shear layer in the HCS may serve as an active site that hosts a chain of ion-scale CSs. This provides new insight into the multiscale structure of HCS reconnection and suggests that flow shear layers may play an important role in generating secondary kinetic-scale structures.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes Parker Solar Probe observations of an HCS crossing on March 29, 2024, identifying 12 ion-scale current sheets (~0.06 sec duration, few ion inertial lengths) embedded within the flow-shear layer at the edge of a reconnection jet. These CSs are classified into three types based on B_M behavior, revealing that the reconnection jet shear layer serves as an active site hosting a chain of kinetic-scale structures.
AI.motivation: To understand the kinetic-scale structure embedded within HCS reconnection regions, specifically ion-scale current sheets and their relationship to flow-shear layers at reconnection jet edges, which remain poorly understood compared to large-scale signatures.
AI.method: Analysis of high-time-resolution magnetic field measurements from Parker Solar Probe on March 29, 2024, focusing on two brief intervals of flow-shear layer at reconnection jet edges, examined in a local LMN coordinate system.
AI.result: Twelve ion-scale CSs identified (average ~0.06 sec duration, few ion inertial lengths), classified into three types: (1) bipolar B_M without B_L bifurcation, (2) bipolar B_M with bifurcated B_L profiles showing plateau structure, (3) strong fluctuations obscuring expected bipolar B_M signature.
AI.conclusion: The reconnection jet shear layer in the HCS may act as an active site hosting a chain of ion-scale CSs, providing new insights into the multiscale nature of HCS reconnection and suggesting flow shear layers play a key role in generating secondary kinetic-scale structures.

title: Improving reddening estimates for RR Lyrae stars in the Gaia bands: a machine learning approach to the PAC(Z) relation
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, 8 tables + appendices. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: RR Lyrae stars are essential tracers of old stellar populations and distance indicators across the Milky Way and nearby galaxies. However, their use as standard candles is limited by uncertainties in extinction, especially in the Gaia bands. In Gaia DR3, absorption values (AG) for fundamental-mode RR Lyrae (RRab) were based on an empirical period-amplitude-color (PAC) relation calibrated on a small sample and on passband transformations. We aim to recalibrate extinction relations for RRab stars and establish, for the first time, analogous relations for first-overtone RR Lyrae (RRc) stars in the Gaia passbands. We used Gaia DR3 photometry and pulsation properties of an all-sky reference sample. Intrinsic colors (G - GRP)0 and (GBP - GRP)0 were derived through sequential feature selection combined with linear regression and bootstrap resampling, ensuring robust estimates of color excess and AG. We recalibrated PACZ relations for RRab stars and derived, for the first time, PAC relations for RRc stars in the Gaia bands. The new relations reproduce intrinsic colors with residual scatters of ~0.03-0.04 mag and provide AG estimates across both pulsation types. Tests on RR Lyrae stars in Galactic globular clusters and dwarf galaxies show consistent AG values, with the PAC(Z) relations based on (GBP - GRP) being more stable than those using (G - GRP). The new relations provide reliable extinction estimates for both RRab and RRc stars, improving on the DR3 implementation and offering a valuable tool for Galactic studies in view of Gaia DR4.

AI.tldr: The paper recalibrates extinction relations for RRab stars and establishes first-ever PAC relations for RRc stars in Gaia passbands, improving extinction estimates for both types using Gaia DR3 data.
AI.motivation: RR Lyrae stars are key tracers of old populations and distance indicators, but uncertainties in extinction, especially in Gaia bands, limit their use as standard candles. The DR3 empirical PAC relation for RRab stars was based on a small sample and passband transformations, motivating a recalibration and extension to RRc stars.
AI.method: The authors used Gaia DR3 photometry and pulsation properties of an all-sky reference sample. Intrinsic colors (G - GRP)0 and (GBP - GRP)0 were derived via sequential feature selection combined with linear regression and bootstrap resampling. PACZ relations for RRab stars were recalibrated, and PAC relations for RRc stars were derived for the first time in Gaia bands.
AI.result: The new relations yield intrinsic color residuals of ~0.03-0.04 mag and provide consistent AG estimates across both RRab and RRc types. Tests on Galactic globular clusters and dwarf galaxies confirmed consistency. PAC(Z) relations based on (GBP - GRP) are more stable than those using (G - GRP).
AI.conclusion: The newly derived PAC(Z) relations provide reliable extinction estimates for both RRab and RRc stars, improving upon the DR3 implementation and offering a valuable tool for Galactic studies ahead of Gaia DR4.

title: Multi-height Diagnosis of MHD Wave Periods and their Propagation in Solar Plages Using IRIS Observations
comment: 26 Pages, 16 figures, Accepted for Publication in Advances in Space Research Journal
summary: This work aims to investigate magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) waves in solar plages at five distinct heights, spanning from photosphere to the upper chromosphere, using spectroscopic observations provided by Interface Region Imaging Spectrograph (IRIS). The dominant period is found to not change within the plages, while, in pores, the dominant period decreases linearly from 4.20 to 3.20 minutes from the photosphere to the upper chromosphere. Furthermore, in the solar plages, cross-wavelet analysis reveals that periods from 2.0 to 6.0 minutes propagate till the upper chromosphere from the photosphere. The periods beyond 6 minutes have a zero/constant phase difference in the photosphere and in the middle chromosphere. Thus, the 6.0-minute period would be considered the cutoff period at these heights. Next, the propagation speeds of MHD waves above solar plages are estimated in the photosphere and chromosphere. Within the limit of uncertainties, the propagation speed in solar plage is close to the sound speed; hence, the waves are slow magnetoacoustic in nature. Lastly, with the help of phase difference analysis, we found that formation heights of Mg ii k2r and Mg ii k3 are underestimated in solar plage, while the formation height of Mn i is overestimated. In case of pores, the formation heights of Mg ii k2r, Mg ii k3, and Mn i are overestimated. Interestingly, in the quiet-Sun (QS), the formation heights of Mn i and Fe i are nearly the same, and also the formation height of Mg ii k3 is similar to the formation height of Mg ii k2r. In conclusion, some important findings are reported in this work, namely, (1) dominant periods at five different heights between the photosphere and chromosphere, (2) estimation of cutoff periods in the photosphere, middle chromosphere, and the upper chromosphere in the plages, and (3) variations in the formation heights of these spectral lines in pore, plage, and QS.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates MHD waves in solar plages at five heights from photosphere to upper chromosphere using IRIS data. Dominant periods are constant in plages but decrease linearly in pores. Waves with periods 2-6 minutes propagate to the upper chromosphere, with 6 minutes as the cutoff period. Propagation speeds are near sound speed, indicating slow magnetoacoustic waves. Formation heights of Mg ii and Mn i lines are reassessed in plages, pores, and quiet-Sun regions.
AI.motivation: To investigate magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) waves in solar plages at five distinct heights from photosphere to upper chromosphere using IRIS spectroscopic observations, and to understand wave propagation characteristics, cutoff periods, and formation heights of spectral lines in different solar features.
AI.method: Spectroscopic observations from IRIS were used to analyze MHD waves at five heights. Cross-wavelet analysis examined period propagation. Phase difference analysis estimated propagation speeds and formation heights of spectral lines (Mg ii k2r, Mg ii k3, Mn i, Fe i) in plages, pores, and quiet-Sun regions.
AI.result: (1) Dominant periods are constant in plages but decrease linearly from 4.20 to 3.20 minutes in pores. (2) Periods 2-6 minutes propagate to upper chromosphere; 6 minutes is the cutoff period. (3) Propagation speeds are close to sound speed (slow magnetoacoustic waves). (4) Formation heights of Mg ii k2r and Mg ii k3 are underestimated in plages but overestimated in pores; Mn i is overestimated in both. In quiet-Sun, Mn i and Fe i heights are similar, and Mg ii k3 and Mg ii k2r heights are similar.
AI.conclusion: Key findings: dominant periods at five heights between photosphere and chromosphere, cutoff period estimation (6 minutes) in plages at different heights, and variations in spectral line formation heights across pore, plage, and quiet-Sun regions.

title: Unveiling the nature of G6096: a likely hierarchical triple system
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures. Accepted for publication in The Astrophysical Journal Letters
summary: G6096 (Gaia DR3 609651611028044544) was recently reported as a wide ($P\sim 450$ days) and eccentric ($e\sim0.18$) binary possibly hosting a massive white dwarf or neutron star. In this work, through analyses of the projected rotational velocity between the blue and red bands, spectral disentangling, joint radial velocity and astrometric fitting, and X-ray emission, we suggest that the system contains additional visible component(s) rather than a compact object. We develop a new approach to reveal the nature of G6096 by jointly modeling the spectral energy distribution, rotational velocity, and astrometric measurements. Finally, we speculate that G6096 is a hierarchical triple main-sequence star system, comprising a primary with a mass of $\sim 0.75\,M_\odot$ orbited by an inner binary consisting of two dwarfs with masses of $\sim 0.62\,M_\odot$ and $\sim 0.40\,M_\odot$, respectively. This method may help reveal a population of triple systems when applied to {\it Gaia} astrometric data, particularly the upcoming DR4.

AI.tldr: G6096, previously thought to be a binary with a compact object (white dwarf or neutron star), is reclassified through multi-faceted analysis as a hierarchical triple main-sequence star system consisting of a ~0.75 M☉ primary orbited by an inner binary of ~0.62 M☉ and ~0.40 M☉ dwarfs.
AI.motivation: To investigate the true nature of G6096, previously reported as a wide eccentric binary possibly containing a massive compact object (white dwarf or neutron star), and to determine whether it actually hosts such an object or has alternative configuration.
AI.method: Multi-faceted analysis including: (1) projected rotational velocity comparison between blue and red bands, (2) spectral disentangling, (3) joint radial velocity and astrometric fitting, (4) X-ray emission analysis, and (5) a novel joint modeling approach combining spectral energy distribution, rotational velocity, and astrometric measurements.
AI.result: The system contains additional visible components rather than a compact object. G6096 is identified as a hierarchical triple main-sequence star system with masses of ~0.75 M☉ (primary), ~0.62 M☉, and ~0.40 M☉ (inner binary dwarfs).
AI.conclusion: The developed joint modeling method may help reveal a population of triple systems when applied to Gaia astrometric data, particularly the upcoming DR4 release.

title: The SKA as a Probe of Heliospheric Turbulence and Radio-wave Propagation Effects
comment: Published in Advancing Astrophysics with the SKA II (AASKAII), 2026 (arXiv:2606.20366). Report-no: AASKAII/Chrysaphi01. Advancing Astrophysics with the SKA II (AASKAII) outlines the transformative scientific advances that will be enabled by the SKA telescopes
summary: Density turbulence in the heliosphere can impact traversing radio photons originating from anywhere in the universe, leading to distortions of both the spectroscopic and imaging properties of the radio sources. Extra-solar radio sources exhibit scintillation and angular broadening, while monochromatic signals from spacecraft are spectrally broadened. It was recently demonstrated that such impacts, referred to as radio-wave propagation effects, are particularly significant in observations of solar radio bursts excited through the plasma emission mechanism. A comparison of detailed observations with simulations is required in order to quantify the radio-wave propagation effects and disentangle the true radio-source properties from the observed ones, enabling the diagnosis of the heliospheric environment. Consequently, theoretical advancements and our ability to quantify the heliospheric turbulence depend on the quality and quantity of available observations. SKA pathfinders, like LOFAR, have been key to the significant progress recently achieved, but have also highlighted areas where the available observing capabilities are lacking. The SKA's unrivalled sensitivity will be crucial in identifying and distinguishing fine and faint radio structures, where our ability to model them defines whether we can accurately describe the heliospheric turbulence and deduce the fundamental properties of the radio sources. The SKA will be an indispensable tool in observing solar radio emissions from the Sun to beyond 1 au, complemented by ground-based interferometers that can reach frequencies down to the ionospheric cut-off at 10 MHz and space-based radio instruments which cover frequencies down to a few kHz.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses how density turbulence in the heliosphere distorts radio waves from various sources (solar bursts, spacecraft signals, extra-solar sources), emphasizing the need for advanced observations and modeling—particularly with the SKA telescope—to disentangle propagation effects from intrinsic source properties and characterize heliospheric turbulence.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how heliospheric density turbulence distorts radio observations, and to disentangle these propagation effects from the true properties of radio sources for accurate diagnosis of the heliospheric environment.
AI.method: Comparison of detailed observations with simulations to quantify radio-wave propagation effects; utilizing SKA pathfinders (e.g., LOFAR) and future SKA observations complemented by ground-based and space-based radio instruments.
AI.result: SKA pathfinders like LOFAR have enabled significant progress but revealed gaps in observing capabilities. The SKA's high sensitivity will be crucial for detecting fine and faint radio structures, enabling accurate modeling of heliospheric turbulence and fundamental source properties.
AI.conclusion: The SKA will be an indispensable tool for observing solar radio emissions from the Sun to beyond 1 au, complemented by ground-based interferometers (down to 10 MHz) and space-based instruments (down to a few kHz), essential for accurately describing heliospheric turbulence and deducing radio source properties.

authors: the Cradle of Life working group
title: Cradle of Life: From the Formation of Stars to Habitable Worlds with the SKAO
comment: Published in Advancing Astrophysics with the SKA II (AASKAII), 2026 (arXiv:2606.20366). Report-no: AASKAII/Bianchi02. Advancing Astrophysics with the SKAII (AASKAII) outlines the transformative scientific advances that will be enabled by the SKA telescopes
summary: This chapter provides an overview of the different science cases covered by the "Cradle of Life" working group, which aims to leverage the capabilities of the Square Kilometre Array Observatory (SKAO) to trace the physical and chemical pathways toward stars and planets formation, how such stars impact their planets, and whether life could exist in such conditions. At its highest frequencies, the SKAO will probe the earliest stages of the raw material fuelling planet formation, while enabling deep, unprecedented searches for prebiotic molecules both in high-mass and solar-type protostars. Concurrently, the lowest frequencies will be deployed to detect and characterize exoplanetary magnetic fields via their auroral radio emission, and the type of space weather a planet experiences from its host star. Across the entire frequency range, the SKA telescopes will conduct systematic searches of technosignatures. Together, these high-impact research areas establish a comprehensive roadmap for the SKAO to uncover the origins of life in the universe.

AI.tldr: The "Cradle of Life" working group outlines how the SKAO will trace pathways from star/planet formation to potential life, using high frequencies to probe planet-forming material and prebiotic molecules, low frequencies to detect exoplanet magnetic fields and stellar space weather, and the full range to search for technosignatures.
AI.motivation: To leverage the Square Kilometre Array Observatory (SKAO) to systematically trace the physical and chemical pathways from star and planet formation to the conditions necessary for life, and to search for signs of life itself.
AI.method: The working group employs the SKAO across its full frequency range: high frequencies probe early-stage planet-forming material and prebiotic molecules in protostars; low frequencies detect exoplanetary magnetic fields via auroral radio emission and characterize stellar space weather; all frequencies are used for technosignature searches.
AI.result: The SKAO will enable deep, unprecedented searches for prebiotic molecules in both high-mass and solar-type protostars, detect and characterize exoplanetary magnetic fields, assess space weather conditions around exoplanets, and conduct systematic technosignature searches.
AI.conclusion: These research areas collectively establish a comprehensive roadmap for the SKAO to uncover the origins of life in the universe.

title: A Homogeneous Spectroscopic and Evolutionary Study of cool HgMn and hot Metallic A Stars
summary: We present a homogeneous spectroscopic and evolutionary analysis of chemically peculiar stars, focusing on HgMn and metallic A (Am) subclasses. Particular emphasis was placed on the coolest HgMn stars and the hottest Am stars, motivated by the long-standing hypothesis that these objects may represent consecutive evolutionary stages of a similar stellar population. High-resolution spectra of 13 HgMn and 14 Am targets were analyzed to derive atmospheric parameters and detailed chemical abundances. While all HgMn stars were confirmed to retain their chemically peculiar nature, only six of the fourteen candidate Am stars exhibit abundance patterns consistent with genuine Am characteristics, highlighting the importance of homogeneous spectroscopic classification. We investigate the temperature dependence of elemental abundances, finding a positive correlation between Mn abundance and $T_{\rm eff}$ and a negative correlation for Ni, supporting the operation of atomic diffusion. Stellar evolutionary models were computed with the MESA code using atmospheric parameters derived from the spectroscopic analysis. The locations of the confirmed HgMn and Am stars in the Hertzsprung--Russell diagram support the possibility that the cool HgMn stars and the hot Am stars may occupy overlapping evolutionary sequences. Although only a limited number of candidate pairs were identified, further analyses using evolutionary models that include atomic diffusion are needed to test the possible connection between the two subclasses.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a homogeneous spectroscopic and evolutionary analysis of HgMn and Am chemically peculiar stars. High-resolution spectra of 13 HgMn and 14 Am targets were analyzed, confirming all HgMn stars as chemically peculiar but only 6 of 14 Am stars showing genuine Am characteristics. Temperature-dependent abundance correlations (Mn positive, Ni negative) support atomic diffusion. Evolutionary models with MESA suggest cool HgMn and hot Am stars may occupy overlapping evolutionary sequences, though further diffusion-inclusive modeling is needed to test a possible evolutionary connection.
AI.motivation: To test the long-standing hypothesis that cool HgMn stars and hot Am stars may represent consecutive evolutionary stages of a similar stellar population, through homogeneous spectroscopic classification and evolutionary modeling.
AI.method: High-resolution spectroscopic analysis of 13 HgMn and 14 Am stars to derive atmospheric parameters and chemical abundances. Stellar evolutionary models computed with the MESA code using derived parameters.
AI.result: All HgMn stars confirmed as chemically peculiar; only 6 of 14 Am candidates show genuine Am abundance patterns. Positive Mn-Teff and negative Ni-Teff correlations found. HR diagram positions of confirmed stars suggest overlapping evolutionary sequences for cool HgMn and hot Am stars, though only limited candidate pairs identified.
AI.conclusion: The overlapping positions in the HR diagram support a possible evolutionary connection between cool HgMn and hot Am stars, but further analyses using evolutionary models incorporating atomic diffusion are needed to confirm this hypothesis.

title: Long-Baseline VLF Observations of Solar Flares from Antarctica
summary: We present long-baseline Very Low Frequency (VLF) observations of solar flare-induced ionospheric disturbances obtained at the Bulgarian Polar Astronomical Observatory (St. Kliment Ohridski Base) on Livingston island, Antarctica. Using continuous VLF transmissions at 21.4 kHz (NPM, Hawaii) and 24.0 kHz (NAA, Maine), propagating over trans-hemispheric paths exceeding 11000 km, we analyze observations of solar flares during the period 24 January--8 February 2025. After removing the strong diurnal signal via superposed epoch analysis, we analyse the flare-related perturbations in VLF amplitude and their correlation with GOES soft X-ray flux for 250 flares of C and M class. The long propagation paths provide enhanced sensitivity to flare-driven changes in D-region ionization. The observations reveal clear, frequency-dependent responses and measurable time delays between X-ray and VLF peaks. These delays, including cases of near-zero or negative lag for stronger events, highlight the role of flare spectral characteristics and D-region recombination processes. Our results demonstrate the scientific value of long-baseline Antarctic VLF observations for detecting and timing GOES-class flares, while also highlighting key limitations, such as background variability and path-dependent propagation effects, which must be quantified for reliable VLF-based flare monitoring.

AI.tldr: This paper presents long-baseline VLF observations from Antarctica (24 Jan–8 Feb 2025) of 250 C/M-class solar flares, using NPM (21.4 kHz) and NAA (24.0 kHz) transmissions. After removing diurnal signals via superposed epoch analysis, they find frequency-dependent amplitude perturbations, measurable X-ray-to-VLF delays (including near-zero/negative lags for stronger events), and demonstrate the value and limitations of Antarctic VLF for flare monitoring.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate the scientific value of long-baseline Antarctic VLF observations for detecting and timing solar flare-induced D-region ionospheric disturbances, leveraging trans-hemispheric paths for enhanced sensitivity.
AI.method: Long-baseline VLF observations at 21.4 kHz (NPM, Hawaii) and 24.0 kHz (NAA, Maine) over trans-hemispheric paths (>11,000 km) to the Bulgarian Polar Astronomical Observatory on Livingston Island, Antarctica. Diurnal signal removed via superposed epoch analysis; flare perturbations correlated with GOES soft X-ray flux for 250 C/M-class flares from 24 Jan–8 Feb 2025.
AI.result: Clear, frequency-dependent VLF amplitude responses; measurable time delays between X-ray and VLF peaks; near-zero or negative lag for stronger events; enhanced sensitivity via long propagation paths.
AI.conclusion: Antarctic long-baseline VLF observations are valuable for detecting and timing GOES-class flares, but background variability and path-dependent propagation effects must be quantified for reliable operational monitoring.

title: The potential broader complex linked to the key Cepheid SV Vul
summary: A new distance was established to the Cepheid SV Vul ($P\simeq45^{d}$) and its host cluster Alicante 13, and is tied in part to deeper UKIDSS-DR6 photometry and Gaia DR3 observations ($d=2.30\pm0.13$ kpc). SV Vul and Alicante 13 possibly belong to a broader coeval stellar complex, which could include Liu-Pang 1738. The clusters and Cepheid share comparable astrometry (e.g., $σ_π\simeq0.02$ mas, $σ_{μ_δ}\simeq0.06$ mas yr$^{-1}$), and similar cluster turnoffs (ages) exist as indicated by ultraviolet UVEX color-color analyses, Gaia XP spectroscopically differentially dereddened color-magnitude diagrams, and Padova isochrones. Yet SV Vul may be comparatively overluminous. Robust radial velocities for both clusters could substantiate certain hypotheses.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a new distance to the Cepheid SV Vul and its host cluster Alicante 13 (d=2.30±0.13 kpc) using deeper UKIDSS-DR6 photometry and Gaia DR3 observations. It suggests they may belong to a larger coeval stellar complex including Liu-Pang 1738, based on matching astrometry, cluster turnoffs from UVEX color-color analyses, Gaia XP dereddened CMDs, and Padova isochrones, though SV Vul appears overluminous. The study calls for robust radial velocities to confirm these hypotheses.
AI.motivation: To improve the distance estimate to the Cepheid SV Vul and investigate its possible association with a broader coeval stellar complex including open clusters Alicante 13 and Liu-Pang 1738.
AI.method: Combined deeper UKIDSS-DR6 photometry and Gaia DR3 astrometry to derive distances; used UVEX color-color analyses, Gaia XP spectroscopically differentially dereddened CMDs, and Padova isochrones for age and turnoff comparison.
AI.result: Distance of 2.30±0.13 kpc for SV Vul and Alicante 13; comparable astrometry (σ_π~0.02 mas, σ_μ_δ~0.06 mas/yr) and similar cluster turnoffs/ages across the three systems, but SV Vul may be overluminous relative to expectations.
AI.conclusion: SV Vul, Alicante 13, and Liu-Pang 1738 likely form part of a coeval stellar complex, but confirmation requires robust radial velocity measurements.

title: Observational Evidence for Counter-helicity Magnetic Reconnection in a Solar Eruption
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astrophysical Journal
summary: Magnetic reconnection between coronal magnetic systems carrying opposite self-helicity may play a role in solar eruptions, but observational evidence remains limited. We investigate an M7.0 flare in NOAA Active Region 13615 on 2024 March 28 using multiwavelength observations and nonlinear force-free field extrapolations. The reconstructed coronal field reveals a low-lying positive-helicity core field beneath an overlying magnetic system of opposite sign. During the eruption, the footpoint connectivity of these two magnetic systems changes markedly: field lines rooted in the western footpoint region change from positive to negative helicity, and the positive-helicity domain is substantially reduced. These changes are accompanied by a remote chromospheric brightening, intermittent EUV stripe-like brightenings extending from the source region toward the remote chromospheric brightening, the subsequent formation of large-scale coronal loops, and a weak outer hard X-ray source located at a footpoint of the core field. Together, these results suggest that the eruption was closely associated with reconnection between the core field and the overlying counter-helicity system, providing observational evidence that counter-helicity reconnection can contribute to the destabilization of eruptive solar magnetic fields.

AI.tldr: This study provides observational evidence that magnetic reconnection between coronal magnetic systems with opposite self-helicity (counter-helicity reconnection) can contribute to solar eruptions, based on multiwavelength analysis of an M7.0 flare on 2024 March 28.
AI.motivation: Although counter-helicity reconnection has been theorized to play a role in solar eruptions, direct observational evidence has been limited.
AI.method: Multiwavelength observations combined with nonlinear force-free field (NLFFF) extrapolations of NOAA Active Region 13615.
AI.result: The core field (positive helicity) and overlying system (negative helicity) showed marked footpoint connectivity changes, including helicity sign reversal in western footpoints, reduction of positive-helicity domain, remote chromospheric brightening, intermittent EUV stripe-like brightenings, formation of large-scale coronal loops, and a weak outer hard X-ray source.
AI.conclusion: The eruption was closely associated with counter-helicity reconnection between the core field and overlying system, providing observational evidence that such reconnection can destabilize eruptive solar magnetic fields.
